records.source_quote: In the field of natural products, three levels of nomenclature are recognized.

records.title: NOMENCLATURE FOR NATURAL PRODUCTS BASED ON PARENT HYDRIDES,
records.source_quote: (ALKALOIDS, STEROIDS, TERPENES, CAROTENES, CORRINOIDS, TETRAPYRROLES, AND SIMILAR COMPOUNDS) This Section is based on the recent publication ‘Revised Section F: Natural Products and Related Compounds, IUPAC Recommendations 1999’ and the additional document ‘Corrections and Modifications 2004’ (ref. 9).

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: BIOLOGICALLY BASED TRIVIAL NAMES
records.source_quote: P-101.1.1 When a compound is isolated from a natural source and a trivial name is required, the name should be based, whenever possible, on the family, genus, or species name of the biological material from which it has been isolated.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Introduction
records.source_quote: Many naturally occurring compounds belong to well defined structural classes, each of which can be characterized by a set of parent structures that are closely related structurally, that is, each can be derived from a fundamental parent structure by one or more defined operations used in systematic substitutive nomenclature (see P-13).

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Structural features allowed for parent structures
records.source_quote: The following rules are applicable to new parent structures.

records.title: A fundamental parent structure should contain acyclic hydrocarbon groups that occur in most of the compounds in the natural product class
records.source_quote: P-101.2.2.4 A fundamental parent structure should be as nearly fully saturated or fully unsaturated in terms of maximum number of noncumulative double bonds (mancude rings), while still representing the level of saturation (or unsaturation) of as many related compounds as possible.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Numbering of parent structures
records.source_quote: P-101.2.3.1 A numbering pattern established among a group of structurally related natural products is used for numbering the skeletal atoms of the fundamental parent structure, providing that all skeletal atoms have been included in the numbering system.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: If no numbering pattern has been become established among the members of a group of structurally related natural products, the fundamental parent structure is numbered according to the following guidelines:
records.source_quote: (a) examine the skeleton to identify the senior ring or ring system, according to P-44.

records.title: Identification of individual rings
records.source_quote: Since locants are used to describe skeletal modifications, as indicated in P-101.3, the identification of individual rings by letters A, B, C, etc., used in the past is no longer recommended, except for the rather special case of the removal of a terminal ring (see P-101.3.6).

records.title: Atomic connector, terminal segment, and bond connector
records.source_quote: For nomenclature purposes, the fundamental parent structures are described by specific arrangements of atoms or groups of atoms called ‘atomic connectors’, ‘terminal segments’ and ‘bond connectors’, that must be taken into consideration in accordance with the additive or subtractive operations modifying a fundamental parent structure.

records.title: Stereochemical configuration of parent structures
records.source_quote: The name of a fundamental parent structure usually implies the absolute configuration of all chirality centers and the configuration of double bonds, when applicable, without further specification.

records.title: Configurations that are different from those in the parent structure

records.title: The stereodescriptors ‘R’ and ‘S’
records.source_quote: The stereodescriptors ‘R’ and ‘S’ are used to describe the absolute configuration not specified by the ‘α/β’ system described above, in accord with the CIP priority system and the rules and conventions described in Chapter 9.

records.title: Introduction
records.source_quote: The skeleton of parent structures can be modified in many ways, contracted, expanded, or rearranged by using operations described in P-13.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Removal of skeletal atoms without affecting the number of rings
examples: In an acyclic portion of a skeletal structure, the skeletal atom removed preferably is the one of an acyclic atomic connector or a terminal segment nearest to the free end of this acyclic portion (this is done in order to maintain as far as
(see P-14.3.5 for use of unprimed and primed locants)
records.source_quote: P-101.3.1.1 The removal of an unsubstituted skeletal atom, saturated or unsaturated, from a ring or of an unsubstituted skeletal atom from a saturated acyclic portion of a fundamental parent structure with its attached hydrogen atom(s) is described by the nondetachable prefix ‘nor’; the loss of two or more such skeletal atoms is indicated by the usual numerical multiplicative prefixes ‘di’, ‘tri’, etc. added before ‘nor’.

records.title: Addition of skeletal atoms without affecting the number of rings
records.source_quote: P-101.3.2.1 The addition of a methylene (–CH2–) group between two skeletal atoms of a fundamental parent structure is described by the nondetachable prefix ‘homo’; the addition of two or more methylene groups is indicated by the numerical multiplicative prefixes ‘di’, ‘tri’, etc.

records.title: Bond formation

records.title: Removal of a terminal ring
records.source_quote: The removal of a terminal ring from a parent structure with the addition of an appropriate number of hydrogen atoms at each junction with the adjacent ring is indicated by the nondetachable prefix ‘des’ followed by the capital italic letter of the ring removed (see P-103.3.5.4 for use of ‘des’ in peptide nomenclature).

records.title: Combination of the prefixes ‘cyclo’, ‘seco’, ‘apo’, ‘homo’, and ‘nor’
examples: Explanation: Podocarpane may be used to generate the ‘seco’ compound with one operation; the same compound may be obtained from labdane but in three operations.

After satisfying P-101.3.7.1 and P-101.3.7.2, the nondetachable prefixes that indicate bond rearrangements (‘cyclo’ and ‘seco’) are cited, followed by those that indicate addition or removal of skeletal atoms (‘homo’ and ‘nor’). If more tha

The preferred semisystematic name results from modifications by only two operations involving the prefixes ‘cyclo’, ‘seco’, ‘apo’, ‘homo’, and ‘nor’. In general nomenclature, more than two operations are allowed. Names are formed by citing
Names in which the order of prefixes is cyclo/seco/apo/homo/nor are preferred to those denoted by the alphabetical order apo/cyclo/homo/nor/seco for the five prefixes.
records.source_quote: Modifications to a fundamental parent structure prescribed by the prefixes in the preceding recommendations (P-101.3.1 through P-101.3.4) may be combined to effect even more drastic changes in structure.

records.title: General methodology
records.source_quote: The principles of skeletal replacement (‘a’) nomenclature, as described in P-15.4 and P-51.4 to modify parent structures, are applied to replace carbon skeletal atoms by heteroatoms, such as O, S, N.

records.title: Skeletal replacement of carbon atoms by heteroatoms
records.source_quote: Heteroatoms are denoted by ‘a’ prefixes that are cited before nondetachable prefixes expressing skeletal modifications in fundamental parent structures, each with a locant to indicate its position; the fixed numbering of the parent structure is maintained.

records.title: Skeletal replacement of heteroatoms by carbon atoms

records.title: Skeletal replacement of heteroatoms by other heteroatoms

records.title: Indicated hydrogen
records.source_quote: When the replacement of a skeletal atom in a portion of a parent structure that is mancude (contains the maximum number of noncumulative double bonds) or an extended conjugated system of double bonds results in the creation of a saturated skeletal position, that position is indicated by the symbolism of indicated hydrogen (see P-14.7 and P-58.2).

records.title: FURTHER ASPECTS OF CONFIGURATIONAL SPECIFICATION
records.source_quote: In addition to the specification of the absolute configuration of fundamental and modified parent structures using ‘α’, ‘β’, ‘ξ’, ‘R’, and ‘S’ stereodescriptors, many other stereochemical features have to be described.

records.title: Inversion of configuration
records.source_quote: Configurational inversion of all chirality centers is indicated by the italicized prefix ‘ent’ (a contracted form for ‘enantio’) placed at the front of the complete name of the compound.

records.title: Racemates
records.source_quote: Racemates are named by citing the italicized stereodescriptor ‘rac’ (an abbreviation for racemo) in front of the whole name of the compound including the prefix ‘epi’, if present.

records.title: Relative configuration
examples: [structures I and II are two conformations of the same 3-hydroxy derivative]
records.source_quote: When the relative, but not the absolute configurational relationships among chirality centers are known, the symbol ‘rel’ in association with R or S (preferred to ‘R*’ and/or ‘S*’) is used in accordance with Rule P-93.5.1.2.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: Nomenclature of carbohydrates is based on the concept of parent monosaccharides having retained names.

records.title: Carbohydrates
records.source_quote: The generic term ‘carbohydrates’ includes monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides as well as substances derived from monosaccharides by reduction of the carbonyl group (alditols), by oxidation of one or more terminal groups to carboxylic acids, or by replacement of one or more hydroxy group(s) by a hydrogen atom, an amino group, a thiol group, or similar heteroatomic groups.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Monosaccharides
records.source_quote: Parent monosaccharides are polyhydroxy aldehydes H-[CHOH]n-CHO or polyhydroxy ketones H-[CHOH]m-CO-[CHOH]n-H with three or more carbon atoms.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The source section contains multiple clauses; normalized predicates keep the section together and mark split points for later compilation.

records.title: Aldoses and ketoses
records.source_quote: Monosaccharides with an aldehydic carbonyl or potential aldehydic carbonyl group are called aldoses; those with a ketonic carbonyl or potential carbonyl group, ketoses.

records.title: Deoxysugars
records.source_quote: Monosaccharides in which an alcoholic hydroxy group has been replaced by a hydrogen atom are called ‘deoxy sugars’.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Amino sugars
records.source_quote: Monosaccharides in which an alcoholic hydroxy group has been replaced by an amino group are called ‘amino sugars’.

records.title: Glycosides
records.source_quote: Glycosides are mixed acetals formally arising by elimination of water between the hemiacetal or hemiketal hydroxy group of a sugar and a hydroxy group of a second compound.

records.title: Oligosaccharides
records.source_quote: Oligosaccharides are compounds in which monosaccharide units are joined by glycosidic linkages.

records.title: Polysaccharides
records.source_quote: ‘Polysaccharide’ (glycan) is the name given to a macromolecule consisting of a large number of monosaccharide (glycose) residues joined to each other by glycosidic linkages.

records.title: PARENT MONOSACCHARIDES
records.source_quote: P-102.2.1 The bases for carbohydrate names are the structures of the parent monosaccharides in their acyclic form.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The source section contains multiple clauses; normalized predicates keep the section together and mark split points for later compilation.

records.title: Numbering parent structures
records.source_quote: The carbon atoms of a monosaccharide are numbered consecutively in such a way that: (1) a (potential) aldehyde group receives the locant 1 (even if a more senior characteristic group is present); (2) the most senior of other characteristic groups expressed in the suffix receives the lowest possible locant, i.e carboxylic acid (derivatives) > (potential) ketonic carbonyl groups.

records.title: The Fischer projection of the acyclic form
records.source_quote: In this representation of a monosaccharide, the carbon chain is written vertically with the lowest numbered carbon at the top, as indicated in P-102.2.2.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: The stereodescriptors ‘D’ and ‘L’
records.source_quote: The simplest aldose is glyceraldehyde.

records.title: The configurational atom
records.source_quote: A monosaccharide is assigned to the ‘D’ or ‘L’ series according to the configuration of the highest numbered chirality center.

records.title: Cyclic forms of monosaccharides
records.source_quote: Most monosaccharides exist as cyclic hemiacetals or hemiketals.

records.title: Ring size
records.source_quote: Out of the various possible heterocyclic ring sizes resulting from hemiacetal or hemiketal formation, those with five and six members, including an oxygen atom, prevail and are discussed in this Section.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Hemiacetal or hemiketal formation is indicated in the Fischer projection of the cyclic form by a long bond joining the original aldehydic or ketonic group to the oxygen atom included in the ring
records.source_quote: Examples: D-glucopyranose D-glucofuranose

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: The Haworth representation
records.source_quote: The Haworth representation is a perspective drawing.

records.title: Anomeric forms; the stereodescriptors ‘α’ and ‘β’
records.source_quote: P-102.3.4.2.1 In the cyclic form, the configuration of the newly created chirality center ‘C-1’ must be expressed.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Configurations ‘α’ and ‘β’ for monosaccharides
records.source_quote: The anomeric reference center in a monosaccharide having a retained name is the configurational atom as defined in P-102.3.3.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Conformation of monosaccharides
records.source_quote: Pyranoses assume conformations that are not planar.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: The Mills depiction
records.source_quote: In this depiction, the main hemiacetal ring is drawn in the plane of the paper.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Stereodescriptors for denoting racemates
records.source_quote: Racemates are indicated by the stereodescriptor ‘DL’.

records.title: Mixtures of anomers
records.source_quote: When a mixture of anomers has to be described, the stereodescriptors ‘α’ and ‘β’ are placed at the front of the name, separated by a comma; in Haworth representations, the symbols H and OH replace the formal bonds at the anomeric carbon atom.

records.title: CHOICE OF PARENT STRUCTURE
examples: (ii) the configurational symbol D rather than L;
(iii) the anomeric stereodescriptor α rather than β;

(d) the parent with the most substituent groups cited as prefixes (bridging substitution for example, 2,3-O-methylene is regarded as multiple substitution for this purpose); the prefixes ‘deoxy’ and ‘anhydro’ are detachable and alphabetized
(e) the parent with the lowest locants for substituent prefixes;
(f) the parent with the lowest locant for the first cited substituent.
records.source_quote: In cases where more than one monosaccharide structure is embedded in a large molecule, a parent structure is chosen on the basis of the following criteria, applied in the order given until a decision is reached: (a) the parent that includes the functional group most senior in the order of classes (see P-41).

records.title: Aldoses
records.source_quote: Names of aldoses are retained or substitutively formed.

records.title: Systematic carbohydrate names
records.source_quote: Systematic carbohydrate names of aldoses are formed from a stem name and a configurational prefix or prefixes.

records.title: Cyclic forms
records.source_quote: For monosaccharides having more than six carbon atoms, the anomeric reference center is the highest numbered atom of the group of chirality centers next to the anomeric center that is involved in the heterocyclic ring and specified by a single configurational prefix.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Classification
records.source_quote: Ketoses are classified as 2-ketoses, 3-ketoses, etc. according to the lowest locant for the position of the (potential) carbonyl group.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Retained names
records.source_quote: Retained names and structures are shown in Table 10.3; the configuration is specified by a ‘D’ or ‘L’ stereodescriptor, as defined in P-102.3.2.

records.title: Systematic carbohydrate names
examples: For ketoses with the carbonyl group at C-3, or at a higher-numbered carbon atom, the carbonyl group is ignored and the set of chirality centers is given the appropriate prefix or prefixes according to Table 10.3.
gluco is earlier in alphanumerical order, see P-102.4 (c)]
when there is a choice, the R configuration
is assigned the lowest locant, see P-14.4 (j)]

records.source_quote: The systematic carbohydrate names of ketoses having four through six carbon atoms are formed from the stem name and the appropriate configurational prefix listed in Table 10.3.

records.title: Deoxy sugars
records.source_quote: P-102.5.3.1 The prefix ‘deoxy’ describes the removal of an ‘oxy’ group, –O–, with rejoining of the hydrogen atom.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Trivial names
records.source_quote: The following names are retained: fucose, quinovose and rhamnose.

records.title: Carbohydrate names derived from retained names
records.source_quote: The prefix ‘deoxy’ is used in combination with a retained name when the deoxygenation does not involve the configuration at any chirality center, for example, 6-deoxy-D-allose.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Systematic carbohydrate names
examples: When the –CH2– group divides the chirality centers into two sets, it is ignored for the purpose of assigning the configurational prefix; the prefix(es) assigned should cover the entire sequence of chirality centers (see aldoses) (see P-102.
records.source_quote: The systematic carbohydrate name consists of the prefix ‘deoxy’, preceded by the appropriate locant and followed by the stem name with such configurational prefixes as necessary to describe the chirality centers present in the deoxy compound.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Amino sugars
records.source_quote: The replacement of a hydroxy group that is not an anomeric hydroxy group of a monosaccharide or a monosaccharide derivative by an amino group is envisaged as substitution of the appropriate hydrogen atom of the corresponding deoxy monosaccharide by an amino group.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Trivial names
records.source_quote: The following glycosamine names are retained.

records.title: Systematic carbohydrate names
records.source_quote: Systematic carbohydrate names are formed, in two steps: in a first step a deoxy sugar is created by deoxygenation at the carbon atom where the amino group is to be introduced by substitution in a second step.

records.title: Thio sugars and other chalcogen analogues
records.source_quote: The replacement of a hydroxy oxygen atom of an aldose or ketose, or of the oxygen atom of the carbonyl group of an acyclic aldose or ketose, by sulfur, selenium or tellurium is indicated by placing the prefix ‘thio’, ‘seleno’ or ‘telluro’, respectively, preceded by the appropriate locant, at the front of the systematic or trivial name of the aldose or ketose.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: O-Substitution
records.source_quote: In order to maintain the integrity of structures and take advantage of retained names to imply the absolute configuration, O-substitution is allowed in carbohydrate nomenclature.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: O-Acetyl and O-alkyl functionalization
records.source_quote: For O-acyl derivatives, names with the acid component cited as a separate word ending in ‘ate’ after the monosaccharide name are preferred to names using O-acyl group prefixes.

records.title: Phosphoric acid esters
records.source_quote: Esters of sugars with phosphoric acid are generally termed ‘phosphates’.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Esters with sulfuric acid
records.source_quote: Esters of sugars with sulfuric acid are named by adding the term ‘sulfate’ after the name of the sugar, with the appropriate locant.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Definitions
records.source_quote: Glycose is a less frequently used term for a monosaccharide.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Names
records.source_quote: Glycosides are named by using functional class nomenclature.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Substitution at a nonterminal carbon atom
records.source_quote: The compound is named as a C-substituted monosaccharide.

records.title: Substitution replacing a nonterminal hydroxy group
records.source_quote: The compound is named as a substituted derivative of a deoxy sugar.

records.title: Substitution at a terminal carbon atom
records.source_quote: Substitution at a terminal carbon atom of a carbohydrate chain creates a new chirality center; the configuration is indicated by the ‘R/S’ system.

records.title: N-Substitution
records.source_quote: Substitution at the –NH2 group of an amino sugar is dealt with in two different ways: (1) The whole substituted amino group is designated as a prefix as in 2-acetamido-2-deoxy-D-glucose or 2-(butylamino)-2-deoxy-D-glucose.

records.title: Alditols
records.source_quote: Alditols are named by changing the ending ‘ose’ in the name of the corresponding aldose into ‘itol’.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Choice of a parent structure
examples: The prefix ‘meso’ may be included in the preferred names of erythritol, ribitol and galactitol. The stereodescriptor ‘D’ or ‘L’ must be given when a derivative of a ‘meso’ form has become asymmetric by substitution. It is also necessary to
(a ‘D’ configuration is senior to ‘L’; see P-102.4)
(a ‘D’ configuration is senior to ‘L’: see P-102.4)
(note that locant ‘1’ must be shifted to the other end of the alditol)
records.source_quote: When the same alditol can be derived from either of two different aldoses, or from an aldose or a ketose, the recommended structure is derived from Rule P-102.4, with the exception of the retained names fucitol and rhamnitol.

records.title: Choice of parent structure for substituted alditols
examples: (b) the lowest locant for the first cited substituent in alphanumerical order, in accordance with criterion (f) in Rule P-102.4.
records.source_quote: The parent structure must have: (a) the lowest locants for substituent prefixes in accordance with criterion (e) in Rule P-102.4; Example: 2,3,5-tri-O-methyl-D-mannitol (not 2,4,5-tri-O-methyl-D-mannitol) (b) the lowest locant for the first cited substituent in alphanumerical order, in accordance with criterion (f) in Rule P-102.4.

records.title: Aminoalditols
records.source_quote: Alditols derived from galactosamine and glucosamine are aminoalditols.

records.title: Aldonic acids
records.source_quote: Aldonic acids are divided into aldotrionic acids, aldotetronic acids, etc. according to the number of carbon atoms in the chain.

records.title: Derivatives of aldonic acids
records.source_quote: Aldonic acids are treated as carboxylic acids having a retained name.

records.source_quote: P-102.5.6.6.3.1 Names of individual ketoaldonic acids are formed by changing the ending ‘ulose’ in the name of the corresponding ketose to ‘ulosonic acid’, preceded by the locant of the ketonic group.

records.source_quote: P-102.5.6.6.4.1 Names of individual uronic acids are formed by changing the ending ‘ose’ in the retained or systematic name of the corresponding aldose to ‘uronic acid’.

records.source_quote: Example: methyl β-D-glucopyranosiduronic acid

records.title: Derivatives of aldaric acids formed by modifying the carboxy group (into esters, amides, hydrazides, nitriles, amic acids, etc.) are named by the methods described in P-102.5.6.6.2.1, P-65 and P-66

records.title: Anhydrides
records.source_quote: Anhydrides are intramolecular or intermolecular derivatives of monosaccharides.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Intramolecular anhydrides
records.source_quote: An intramolecular ether (commonly called an intramolecular anhydride), formally arising by elimination of water from two hydroxy groups of a single molecule of a monosaccharide (aldose, ketose) or monosaccharide derivative, is named by adding the detachable prefix ‘anhydro’, preceded by a pair of locants identifying the two hydroxy groups, to the name of the monosaccharide.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Intermolecular anhydrides
examples: [α-D-fructopyranose is cited first; according to P-102.4 (c), α precedes β]
records.source_quote: The cyclic product of condensation of two monosaccharide molecules with elimination of two molecules of water (commonly called an intermolecular anhydride) is named by placing the term ‘dianhydride’ after the names of the two parent monosaccharides.

records.source_quote: P-102.6.1.1.1 Substituent groups formed by removal of the anomeric hydroxy group from a cyclic monosaccharide are named by replacing the final letter ‘e’ of the monosaccharide name by ‘yl’.

records.title: N-Glycosyl compounds (glycosylamines)

records.title: C-Glycosyl compounds

records.title: Glycosyl halides, pseudohalides and esters
records.source_quote: Glycosyl halides and pseudohalides are named by using functional class nomenclature, by adding, as a separate word, the class name ‘chloride’, ‘isocyanate’, etc. to the name of the appropriate glycosyl group.

records.title: DISACCHARIDES AND OLIGOSACCHARIDES
records.source_quote: Names of disaccharides and oligosaccharides are formed by the principles, rules, and conventions described above for monosaccharides.

records.source_quote: Disaccharides which can be regarded as formed by elimination of one molecule of water from two glycosidic (anomeric) hydroxy groups, are named as glycosyl glycosides.

records.title: Disaccharides with a free hemiacetal group
records.source_quote: Disaccharides which can be regarded as formed by elimination of one molecule of water from one glycosidic (anomeric) hydroxy group and one alcoholic hydroxy group, are named as glycosylglycoses.

records.title: Oligosaccharides
records.source_quote: Oligosaccharides are multicomponent saccharides generally with more than two monosaccharide units According to the number of units, they are called trisaccharides, tetrasaccharides, etc.

records.title: Oligosaccharides without a free hemiacetal group

records.title: Oligosaccharides with a free hemiacetal group
records.source_quote: An oligosaccharide of this type is named as a glycosyl[glycosyl]nglycose, the ‘glycose’ portion being the parent.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: This Section describes the nomenclature of amino acids that constitute the building blocks of peptides and proteins.

records.title: Retained names of the ‘common’ amino acids
records.source_quote: The retained names of the α-amino acids that are commonly found in proteins and are represented in the genetic code, together with their systematic names, symbols (3-letter and/or 1-letter), and formulas, are given in Table 10.4.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.
The source section contains multiple clauses; normalized predicates keep the section together and mark split points for later compilation.

records.title: Retained names of ‘less common’ amino acids
records.source_quote: Several other less common trivial names and their symbols are described in Table 10.5.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The source section contains multiple clauses; normalized predicates keep the section together and mark split points for later compilation.

records.title: Systematic substitutive names
records.source_quote: When not denoted by a retained name, amino acids receive systematic substitutive names constructed by applying the principles, rules and conventions of substitutive nomenclature.

records.title: Numbering of α-amino carboxylic acids
records.source_quote: In acyclic amino acids, the carbon atom of the carboxy group next to the carbon atom carrying the amino group is numbered ‘1’.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: The stereodescriptors ‘D’ and ‘L’
records.source_quote: The absolute configuration at the α-carbon atom of the α-amino carboxylic acids is designated by the stereodescriptor ‘D’ or ‘L’ to indicate a formal relationship to ‘D- or L-glyceraldehyde’.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Use of CIP stereodescriptors
records.source_quote: Stereodescriptors ‘R’ and ‘S’ are used for designating the configuration at centers other than the ‘α-C’ atom, while preserving stereodescriptors ‘D’ and ‘L’ for the ‘α-C’ atom to maintain homogeneity of stereodescriptors in peptides (see P-103.3.4).

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Use of the prefix ‘allo’
records.source_quote: The prefix ‘allo’ is used to modify the retained names ‘isoleucine’ and ‘threonine’ when the configuration at ‘C-3’ has been inverted.

records.title: DERIVATIVES OF AMINO ACIDS
records.source_quote: Retained names are used to generate names of salts, esters and acyl groups, and those of derivatives formed by substitution on carbon and nitrogen atoms or functionalization on oxygen and sulfur atoms.

records.title: System for denoting locants
records.source_quote: It is recommended to use ‘N’, ‘O’ and ‘S’ locants with the numerical locant of the attached carbon atom as a superscript to describe substitution on a nitrogen, oxygen, or sulfur atom when more than one is present.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Names of substituent groups
records.source_quote: When α-amino carboxylic acids must be cited as substituent groups in the presence of characteristic groups having seniority for citation as suffix, prefixes are formed according to the following principles.

records.title: Substituent groups with the free valence on a carbon atom
records.source_quote: Substituent groups with the free valence on a carbon atom of an α-amino carboxylic acid are formed according to the rules, principles and conventions of substitutive nomenclature as given in the previous chapters of these recommendations.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Substituent groups with the free valence on a nitrogen atom
examples: When there is more than one nitrogen atom in the amino acid, the use of a locant of the form ‘Nx’ is recommended.
records.source_quote: Substituent groups with the free valence on a nitrogen atom of an α-amino carboxylic acid formed by the removal of a hydrogen atom from the amino group of an amino acid may be named by changing the ending ‘e’ of the name of the α-amino acid into ‘o’, by adding the letter ‘o’ to tryptophan and by constructing the names asparto and glutamo, from aspartic acid and glutamic acid, respectively.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Derivatives formed by substitution
examples: [an acid, azonous acid for (HO)2NH, is senior to an ester; see P-67.1.1.1]
records.source_quote: Retained names are used to indicate carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur substitution.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Ionization of characteristic groups
records.source_quote: P-103.2.4.1 The predominant form of a monoamino monocarboxylic acid in a neutral solution (pH 7) is R-CH(NH3+)-COO– rather than R-CH(NH2)-COOH.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Further forms are required for amino acids that contain two amino groups or two carboxylic groups
examples: glutamate (unqualified, the name ’glutamate’ means the dianion)
records.source_quote: P-103.2.4.3.1 The singly charged anions of aspartic and glutamic acids (strictly each has one positive charge and two negative charges, but this nomenclature refers to net charge) and may be distinguished from the doubly charged anions by placing the charge after the name or by stating the number of neutralizing cations.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Zwitterionic amino acids are named in two ways:
records.source_quote: (1) by prefixing the name of the cationic substituent group into that of the anionic parent (see P-74.1.3) and using CIP stereodescriptors to describe the configuration; (2) by adding the term ‘zwitterion’ to the name of the amino acid.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Acyl groups
records.source_quote: Acyl groups derived from organic acids, for example H2N-CHR-CO–, are named by changing the ending ‘ine’ (or ‘an’ in tryptophan) into ‘yl’, for example alanyl, valyl, tryptophyl. ‘Cysteinyl’ is used instead of ‘cysteyl’; ‘cystyl’ is derived from ‘cystine’ (see P-103.1.1.2).

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Esters
records.source_quote: Esters of amino acids, R-CO-OR′, are formed by the general method using the ‘ate’ ending obtained by replacing the ‘ic acid’ ending or the final letter ‘e’ of the retained name (or adding the ending ‘ate’ to the name tryptophan) and the name of the substituent group R′.

records.title: Amides, anilides, hydrazides, and other nitrogenous analogues
examples: The 4-amide of aspartic acid has its own name, asparagine, and the 5-amide of glutamic acid is glutamine (see Table 10.4). Their 1-amides are named as follows:
The 1-amides of aspartic acid and glutamic acid are named as follows:
Names of anilides are formed by N-substitution of the amide group by the phenyl group or a substituted phenyl group. The ending ‘anilide’, in place of ‘amide’, may also be used.
Substitution on nitrogen atoms in amides of amino acids is expressed systematically by the methods described for amides (P-66.1.1.3) and amines (P-62.2.2.1).
records.source_quote: Amides, anilides, hydrazides, and other nitrogenous analogues derivatives derived from amino acids are named systematically.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, and nitriles
records.source_quote: Alcohols, aldehydes, ketones and nitriles corresponding to amino acids with retained trivial names are named systematically by using the principles, rules and conventions of substitutive nomenclature.

records.title: NOMENCLATURE OF PEPTIDES
records.source_quote: Nomenclature of peptides is highly specialized and well documented in ref. 18.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Definitions
records.source_quote: Peptides are amides derived from two or more amino carboxylic acid molecules (the same or different) by formation of a covalent bond from the carbonyl carbon of one to the nitrogen atom of another with formal loss of water.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Names of peptides
records.source_quote: To name peptides, the names of acyl groups ending in ‘yl’ (see P-103.2.5) are used.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Symbols of peptides
records.source_quote: The peptide glycylglycylglycine is symbolized as Gly-Gly-Gly.

records.title: Indication of configuration in peptides
examples: The symbol ‘DL’ is used to indicate a racemic mixture when one chirality center is present as indicated in P-103.1.3.1 but is not allowed in peptides, as its presence indicates the presence of diastereoisomers in unknown proportions. The it
records.source_quote: The stereodescriptor ‘L’ is not indicated in the names nor in the symbolic representation of peptides composed of amino acids listed in Table 10.4.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Modification of named peptides
records.source_quote: It is often convenient to specify the structure of a peptide by reference to a named sequence to which it is a variant.

records.title: Replacement of residues
records.source_quote: In a peptide of trivial name bradykinin, if the qth amino acid residue, starting from the ‘N-terminal’ end of the chain is replaced by the amino acid ‘Xaa’, the name of the modified peptide is [q-aminoacid]bradikinin and the abbreviated form is [Xaaq]bradykinin.

records.title: The specification of a sequence requires the species as well as the peptide to be named. If so, the name of the species must be attached, in parentheses, to the name of the peptide whenever a modifying prefix is present
records.source_quote: Examples: Chain A Chain B [AlaB12]insulin (human) Chain A Chain B [B3-lysine,B29-glutamic acid]insulin (human) P-103.3.5.1.2 The replacement of an amino acid residue by its enantiomer is expressed as follows.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Extension of the peptide chain
records.source_quote: The compounds obtained by the extension of a peptide at either the N-terminus or the C-terminus are designated by the kind of names and abbreviations shown below.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Extension at the ‘N-terminus’
records.source_quote: Examples: valylbradykinin Val-bradykinin valylglycylbradykinin Val-Gly-bradykinin

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Extension at the ‘C-terminus’
examples: (this insulin is modified by replacement in Chain A and extension at the C-terminus in Chain B)
records.source_quote: Examples: bradykininylleucine bradykininyl-Leu Chain A Chain B insulin-B30-yl-L-argininyl-L-arginine (human) Chain A Chain B [A21-glycine]insulin-B30-yl-L-argininyl-L-arginine (human) (this insulin is modified by replacement in Chain A and extension at the C-terminus in Chain B)

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Insertion of residues
examples: Multiple insertions, and insertion of a maximum of two residues, together in the same place in the chain are shown by a logical extension of this recommendation. Thus the insertion into the peptide bradykinin of threonine between residues 1
records.source_quote: In peptide nomenclature, the prefix ‘endo’ (nonitalic) is used to denote the insertion of an amino acid residue in a well identified position in the peptide.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Removal of residues
records.source_quote: The subtractive prefix ‘des’, in peptide nomenclature, is used to denote the removal of an amino acid residue from any position in a peptide structure.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Cyclic peptides
records.source_quote: Cyclic peptides include rings generated from a peptide (acyclic peptide) by formation of a peptide or ester bond, by a disulfide link, or by a new carbon-carbon, carbon-nitrogen, carbon-oxygen, or carbon-sulfur bond (excluding esters and amides).

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: The nomenclature of cyclitols is described in the document entitled ‘Nomenclature of Cyclitols, Recommendations 1973’ (ref. 39).

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: DEFINITIONS
records.source_quote: Cyclitols are cycloalkanes in which three or more ring atoms are each substituted with one hydroxy group.

records.title: NAME CONSTRUCTION
examples: (a) to the substituents considered as a numerical series, without regard to configuration;
(b) if one set of the substituents is more numerous than the other, to the more numerous;
(c) if the sets are equally numerous and one of them can be denoted by lower numbers, to that set;
(d) to substituents other than unmodified hydroxy groups;
(e) to the substituent first cited in alphanumerical order;
(f) to those designations that lead to an ‘L’ rather than a ‘D’ configuration, as determined by the method of P-104.2.1 above (applies to meso compounds only).

The positional numbers are described by a fractional expression in which the numerator is the set of substituents with the lowest locants, arranged in ascending order, and the denominator is the other set.
records.source_quote: Various methods are recommended for naming cyclitols.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The source section contains multiple clauses; normalized predicates keep the section together and mark split points for later compilation.

records.title: Derivatives of inositols
reject: reject_name_form: 2-carboxy-2-deoxy-myo-inositol; COOH is senior to OH
examples: not 2-carboxy-2-deoxy-myo-inositol; COOH is senior to OH
records.source_quote: Inositols are modified in the same way as carbohydrates to generate names for derivatives.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Derivatives of cyclitols other than inositols
records.source_quote: Names for derivatives of cyclitols other than inositols are all constructed by applying the principles, rules, and conventions of substitutive nomenclature described in Chapters P-1 through P-9.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: The nomenclature of nucleosides is exemplified in the document entitled ‘Abbreviations and Symbols for Nucleic Acids, Polynucleotides and their Constituents’ (ref. 47).

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: RETAINED NAMES OF NUCLEOSIDES
records.source_quote: The following names are retained. adenosine guanosine inosine xanthosine cytidine thymidine uridine

records.title: SUBSTITUTION ON NUCLEOSIDES
examples: (the replacement of a hydroxy group by a fluorine atom at the same position is allowed)
records.source_quote: P-105.2.1 Nucleosides having retained names can be fully substituted on the purine or pyrimidine ring.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: In the presence of a characteristic group of higher priority than pseudoketone, normal substitutive nomenclature principles are applied
records.source_quote: Examples: 3-[4-(methylamino)-2-oxo-1-β-D-ribofuranosyl-1,2-dihydropyrimidin-5-yl]propanoic acid 2′,3′-dideoxyguanosine-2′,3′-diyl carbonate (see P-101.7.4) guanosine cyclic-2′,3′-carbonate

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: Names of nucleotides are exemplified in the document entitled ‘Abbreviations and Symbols for Nucleic Acids, Polynucleotides and their Constituents’ (ref. 47).

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: RETAINED NAMES
records.source_quote: The following are traditional names for esters of nucleosides with phosphoric acid.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: NUCLEOSIDE DIPHOSPHATES AND TRIPHOSPHATES
records.source_quote: Diphosphate, triphosphate, etc. esters of nucleosides are named by citing a phrase such as diphosphate, after the name of the nucleoside.

records.title: DERIVATIVES OF NUCLEOTIDES
records.source_quote: P-106.3.1 Derivatives of nucleotides having retained names are named in the same manner as the corresponding nucleoside, i.e., they can be fully substituted on the purine or pyrimidine ring and the ribofuranosyl component may be modified as prescribed for carbohydrates (see P-102.5).

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Analogues of nucleoside di- and polyphosphates can be named by the functional replacement techniques applicable to di- and polyphosphoric acids (see P-67.2)
examples: (this name preserves the integrity of the nucleotide name; see P-101.4.3)
records.source_quote: Examples: adenosine 5′-(trihydrogen 2-thiodiphosphate) guanosine 5′-[hydrogen (phosphonomethyl)phosphonate] guanosine 5′-(trihydrogen methylenediphosphonate) guanosine 5′-(trihydrogen 2-carbadiphosphate) (this name preserves the integrity of the nucleotide name; see P-101.4.3) P-106.3.3 In the presence of a characteristic group of higher priority than the phosphoric acid residue, normal substitutive nomenclature principles are applied.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Oligonucleotides are named using the prefix names derived from the traditional names for the nucleotides
records.source_quote: Example: 2′-deoxyguanylyl-(3′→5′)-2′-deoxyuridylyl-(3′→5′)-2′-deoxyguanosine

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: When a phosphorothioic acid is used (HS-P in place of HO-P), the prefix P-thio is added at the front of the name of the nucleotide
records.source_quote: Example: 2′-deoxy-P-thioguanylyl-(3′→5′)-2′-deoxy-P-thiouridylyl-(3′→5′)-2′-deoxyguanosine

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: INTRODUCTION
records.source_quote: The nomenclature of lipids, phospholipids, and glycolipids has been published in 1976 (ref. 48); the nomenclature of glycolipids was revised in 1997 (ref. 50).

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: DEFINITIONS
records.source_quote: ‘Lipids’ is a loosely defined term for substances of biological origin that are soluble in nonpolar solvents.

records.title: GLYCERIDES
examples: Phospholipids are lipids containing phosphoric acid as mono- or diesters, including ‘phosphatidic acids’ and ‘phosphoglycerides’.

Phosphatidic acids are derivatives of glycerol in which one hydroxy group, commonly but not necessarily primary, is esterified with phosphoric acid, and the other two hydroxy groups are esterified with fatty acids.

Phosphoglycerides are phosphoric diesters, esters of phosphatidic acids, generally having a polar head group (–OH or –NH2) on the esterified alcohol which typically is 2-aminoethanol (not ‘ethanolamine’), choline, glycerol, inositol, or ser
records.source_quote: Glycerides are esters of glycerol (propane-1,2,3-triol) with fatty acids.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Rule execution requires a structured source table or allowlist.

records.title: Phosphatidic acids have the following generic structure:
records.source_quote: a 3-sn-phosphatidic acid (for a discussion and examples of ‘sn’ see P-107.3.2) In general, the 3-sn-phosphatidic acids are simply called phosphatidic acids. a 2-phosphatidic acid The name of the monovalent acyl group is ‘phosphatidyl’, a retained name. phosphatidyl

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Configuration of phosphatidic acids
records.source_quote: In order to designate the configuration of glycerol derivatives, the carbon atoms of glycerol are numbered by a method referred to as ‘stereospecific numbering’.

records.title: Phosphatidylserines
records.source_quote: The term ‘phosphatidylserines’ is used to describe the acyl derivatives of phosphatidic acids whose phosphorus acid component is esterified with the amino acid ‘serine’, usually L-serine.

records.title: Phosphatidylcholines
records.source_quote: The term ‘phosphatidylcholines’ is used to describe the acyl derivatives of phosphatidic acids whose phosphorus acid component is esterified with choline.

records.title: Phosphatidylethanolamine
records.source_quote: The term ‘phosphatidylethanolamines’ [more correctly designated as ‘phosphatidyl(amino)ethanols’] is used to describe the acyl derivatives of phosphatidic acids whose phosphorus acid component is esterified with 2-aminoethanol.

records.title: Phosphatidylinositols
records.source_quote: The term ‘phosphatidylinositols’ is used to describe the acyl derivatives of phosphatidic acids whose phosphorus acid component is esterified with an inositol molecule.

records.title: Definitions
records.source_quote: The term ‘glycolipid’ designates any compound containing one or more monosaccharide residues bound by a glycosidic linkage to a hydrophobic moiety such as an acyl glycerol, a sphingoid (a long chain aliphatic amino alcohol), a ceramide (an N-acyl-sphingoid), or a prenylphosphate.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Glycoglycerolipids
records.source_quote: Specific compounds are named on the basis of the parent glycerol, whose configuration is specifically numbered as indicated in P-107.3.2] Example: 3-O-β-D-galactopyranosyl-1,2-di-O-octadecanoyl-sn-glycerol (2S)-3-(β-D-galactopyranosyloxy)propane-1,2-diyl di(octadecanoate)

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.
The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Glycosphingolipids
records.source_quote: P-107.4.3.1 Names are formed by using the retained name ‘sphinganine’ for the aliphatic amino alcohol having the described absolute configuration.

implementation_requirements.resolution: Cross-referenced Blue Book rules are represented as dependency edges.

records.title: Ceramides
records.source_quote: Ceramides are N-acylsphingoids.

implementation_requirements.resolution: The draft note is represented as an explicit compilation requirement.

records.title: Neutral glycosphingolipids
records.source_quote: A neutral glycosphingolipid is a carbohydrate-containing derivative of a sphingoid or ceramide.
